title: APPENDIX — Quick-reference summary (not for reading aloud)

lines.text: APPENDIX — Quick-reference summary (not for reading aloud)
Core analogies used in this lecture:

Synthetic embryos = assemble pluripotent cells with the right signaling cocktail and they SELF-ORGANIZE into embryo-like structures — you don't sculpt them, you give them the cues and they self-assemble The trophoblast missing piece = for a long time we had embryonic stem cells but couldn't model the trophoblast (placenta precursor); Niakan and Okae finally derived stable human trophoblast stem cells Blastoid = iPSCs + trophoblast stem cells, gelled together with the right cocktail, gives you a

structure that looks and behaves like a blastocyst Hanna's mouse synthetic embryo = the most striking demonstration — mouse model develops to day 8.5 in vitro with heart beating and brain forming, from stem cells alone The 14-day rule = the longstanding embryo-research ethical limit — primitive streak by day 14, gastrulation, individuation; synthetic embryos blur whether the 14-day clock applies ISSCR 2021 update = removed the 14-day rule from forbidden; case-by-case review now; the ethics is
evolving in real time Defining "embryo" = the field's open question — if a structure has trophoblast + ICM + extraembryonic and can develop, is it an embryo?

Different jurisdictions, different answers Comparison with organoids = cerebral organoids are clearly not embryos; synthetic embryos clearly mimic embryos; somewhere in between the line is fuzzy and ethics committees are catching up Two-axis framework = developmental progress (how far through development the structure goes) vs morphological similarity (how closely it resembles a real embryo at that stage); ethics calibrated to both axes
Three core ingredients for an integrated synthetic embryo:

Pluripotent stem cells (ESCs or iPSCs) — to make the inner cell mass / epiblast Trophoblast stem cells — to make the outer layer / placenta precursor Extraembryonic endoderm (XEN) cells — to make the hypoblast / yolk sac
Three core limitations of current synthetic embryos:

Low efficiency — only a small fraction of starting aggregates form proper structures Anatomical drift — proportions and architecture can deviate from real embryos Developmental ceiling — current best in mouse is ~day 8.5; in human, early post-implantation
Key historical milestones:

1981 — Mouse embryonic stem cells derived (Evans/Kaufman; Martin) 1998 — Human embryonic stem cells derived (Thomson); Mouse trophoblast stem cells derived (Rossant) 2006 — Yamanaka derives iPSCs from mouse fibroblasts 2007 — Yamanaka and Thomson independently derive human iPSCs 2014 — Niakan analyzes human blastocyst signaling, points the way to human TS cells 2017 — Zernicka-Goetz lab begins publishing ETX embryoid work in mouse 2018 — Rivron publishes first mouse blastoids (Nature); Okae

derives human trophoblast stem cells (Cell Stem Cell) 2021 — Polo lab and Hanna lab independently publish human blastoids (Nature, same issue, March 2021) 2021 — ISSCR updates guidelines: 14-day rule moved from forbidden to case-by-case review 2022 — Hanna lab publishes mouse synthetic embryos developing to day 8.5 with heart and brain (Cell) 2023 — Zernicka-Goetz and Hanna labs independently publish human ETX-style models through early post-implantation 2023+ — Continuing extension of time
window, refinement of protocols, ongoing ethical and policy development
Key researchers and their contributions:

Nicolas Rivron — first mouse blastoids; founder of the formal synthetic embryo era Magdalena Zernicka-Goetz — mouse and human ETX embryoids; ethics advocate Jacob Hanna — naive pluripotent stem cells; mouse synthetic embryos to day 8.5; human ETX models Jose Polo — human blastoids from iPSCs Kathy Niakan — single-cell analysis of human blastocyst signaling Hiroaki Okae and Takahiro Arima — first stable human trophoblast stem cells Janet Rossant — first mouse trophoblast stem cells (1998) Shinya

Yamanaka — iPSC reprogramming; thoughtful ethics commentary Jürgen Knoblich — cerebral organoid pioneer with relevant ethics views Ali Brivanlou — long-term embryo culture; pre-2016 blastocyst-stage work
Three positions on what counts as an embryo:

Origin-based — only products of fertilization are embryos; synthetic structures are models Potential-based — embryos are anything that could develop into a person; embryo status depends on developmental capacity Similarity-based — embryos are anything sufficiently similar to embryos morphologically and molecularly; treat by resemblance
Key applications:

Understanding early human development (the black box of weeks 1-4) Modeling genetic diseases of early development Drug safety screening on early development Improving IVF success rates Studying placentation and pregnancy disorders Indirect benefit to regenerative medicine
Legal landscape highlights:

UK — strong oversight under HFEA; embryo research up to 14 days in law; synthetic embryo status being clarified US — patchwork; no federal law; Dickey-Wicker limits federal funding for embryo destruction; state-by-state variation China — generally permissive; oversight variable; significant research output Japan — updated guidelines aligned with ISSCR; major research contributor Germany — historically restrictive; synthetic embryo status being worked out ISSCR — global influence through 2021
guidelines; case-by-case review now recommended
Where the field is heading:

Extending the time window (mouse past day 8.5, human past early post-implantation) Solving vascularization to allow larger, more sustained structures Integrating with maternal-tissue models (endometrial organoids) for implantation studies Higher-fidelity recapitulation of real embryo biology Organogenesis-in-a-dish — early organs forming in synthetic embryo context Distant: in vitro gestation; converging with artificial placenta work Evolving regulatory and ethical frameworks tracking the
technological frontier
